front.title: Forward Error Correction for Media over QUIC (MoQ)

abstract.t: This document specifies a mechanism for transmitting Forward Error Correction (FEC) repair data alongside source media in Media over QUIC (MoQ) sessions. It defines signaling for FEC configuration via catalog extensions, conventions for repair track naming, and the format of repair objects. The mechanism supports RaptorQ (RFC 6330), Reed-Solomon (RFC 5510), and XOR-based FEC schemes, enabling receivers to recover from packet loss without retransmission latency. This specification is designed for MoQ unicast delivery over QUIC streams and datagrams. Multicast delivery of FEC-protected media is defined separately in .

section.name: Introduction
section.t: Media over QUIC (MoQ) provides a publish/subscribe transport for real-time media. While QUIC provides reliable delivery through retransmission, the latency incurred by retransmission can be unacceptable for ultra-low-latency (ULL) applications such as live sports, gaming, and interactive broadcasts.

Forward Error Correction (FEC) enables receivers to recover lost packets using redundant repair data, without waiting for retransmissions. This is particularly valuable in wireless environments where burst packet loss is common.

FEC is particularly important for MoQ sessions using the Datagram forwarding preference per , where QUIC provides no retransmission. In datagram mode, FEC is the sole loss recovery mechanism, making repair track subscription RECOMMENDED for subscribers using unreliable transport.
This document defines:

ol.li: A catalog extension for FEC parameter signaling
A naming convention for repair tracks
The format of repair objects containing FEC symbols
A MoQ extension header for FEC source block identification
Interleaving strategies to protect against burst loss

section.t: The mechanism is designed to complement existing MoQ media packaging formats including CMAF , LOC , and MMT by operating as a separate protection layer.
This document addresses MoQ unicast delivery over QUIC. Multicast delivery of FEC-protected media, including the condensed multicast packet format and multi-path FEC, is defined in .

section.name: Terminology
section.t: The key words "MUST", "MUST NOT", "REQUIRED", "SHALL", "SHALL NOT", "SHOULD", "SHOULD NOT", "RECOMMENDED", "NOT RECOMMENDED", "MAY", and "OPTIONAL" in this document are to be interpreted as described in BCP 14 when, and only when, they appear in all capitals, as shown here.

dl.dd: A MoQ track carrying media data (e.g., video, audio).
A MoQ track carrying FEC repair symbols for a corresponding source track.
A sequence of K source symbols over which FEC encoding is performed.
A fixed-size unit of source data used for FEC encoding.
A symbol generated by the FEC encoder that can be used to recover lost source symbols.
The combination of K source symbols and P repair symbols.
The number of media units (e.g., frames, chunks) spanned by a single source block.
Parameters required to configure a RaptorQ decoder, as defined in .
A zero-based index identifying a specific source block within a session.

section.name: FEC Algorithms
section.t: XOR (0x01): Simple XOR-based FEC. Each repair symbol is the XOR of all source symbols in the block. Can recover exactly one lost symbol per block.
RaptorQ (0x02): RaptorQ fountain code per . Can recover from loss of any symbols as long as K symbols (source or repair) are received. Recommended for most applications.

Reed-Solomon (0x03): Reed-Solomon erasure code over GF(2^8) per . Can recover up to P lost symbols. Suitable for applications requiring exact recovery guarantees or interoperability with ATSC 3.0 systems using RS FEC.

section.name: RaptorQ Object Transmission Information
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is RaptorQ (0x02), the OTI field contains the concatenation of the 8-byte Common FEC OTI (Section 3.3.2 of ) and the 4-byte Scheme-Specific FEC OTI (Section 3.3.3 of ), totaling 12 bytes:
section.artwork: RaptorQ OTI { Transfer Length (40), Reserved (8), Symbol Size (16), Number of Source Blocks (8), Number of Sub-Blocks (16), Symbol Alignment (8), }
section.t: Note: For MoQ usage, Transfer Length is typically computed per source block from the cumulative size of source objects in the block.

section.name: Reed-Solomon Parameters
section.t: When FEC Algorithm is Reed-Solomon (0x03), the OTI field contains:

section.name: Repair Object Format Selection
section.t: The format of repair object payloads is determined by the repair track's packaging type in the catalog. Each packaging type defines its own repair object layout:

tr.td: Raw repair symbol, SBN/ESI from MoQ framing ()
LOC object with repair symbol payload ()

section.t: The repair track's packaging MUST match the source track's packaging when the source uses a format with native FEC support (mmtp, loc). When the source uses cmaf packaging, the repair track uses condensed packaging because CMAF does not define a native repair framing.
Receivers determine the repair format by inspecting the repair track's packaging field in the catalog. No separate "repair container" signaling is needed -- the packaging field IS the signal.

section.name: Catalog Fields
section.t: MoQ catalogs MAY include FEC configuration as an extension field at the track level. The fec object is a custom extension per Section 3.1; parsers that do not support FEC MUST ignore it.

The repair track is a regular catalog track whose packaging field indicates the repair object format (). In this example, the source uses LOC packaging and the repair uses condensed packaging -- repair objects contain raw repair symbols with SBN/ESI derived from MoQ transport framing.
Field definitions for the fec object:

dl.dd:
FEC scheme identifier.
"none": No FEC
"xor": Simple XOR
"raptorq": RaptorQ per RFC 6330
"reed-solomon": Reed-Solomon per RFC 5510
K value - source symbols per FEC block.
P value - repair symbols per FEC block.
Number of media units per FEC block. Default is 1.
Track name for repair symbols, following the convention in .

section.t: Media properties (codec, width, height, framerate, bitrate, samplerate, channelConfig) are defined in the selectionParams object per Section 3.2.17 and are not repeated here.

section.name: Track Naming
section.t: For a source track with name components [namespace, track_name], the corresponding repair track MUST be named:
Examples:
This convention provides a predictable default. The catalog repairTrack field () is authoritative; publishers MAY use different names.

section.name: Subscription Model
section.t: Subscribers SHOULD subscribe to the source track first, then subscribe to the repair track if:

ol.li: The catalog indicates FEC is available for the source track, AND

section.t: Publishers MUST NOT require subscription to repair tracks. Repair tracks are optional and subscribers MAY choose to rely solely on QUIC retransmission.
Subscribers discover FEC availability and repair track names from the catalog ().

section.name: Condensed Repair Format
section.t: This section defines the condensed repair format, used when the repair track's packaging is "condensed" in the catalog. This is the default repair format for CMAF source tracks, which do not have a native FEC framing.
When the repair track uses a different packaging (e.g., "mmtp" per , or "loc" per ), repair object payloads follow the packaging-specific format and this section does not apply.
Each MoQ object on a repair track contains exactly one repair symbol. The object payload is the raw repair symbol data (Symbol Size bytes), with no additional header.
SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ transport framing:
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id
section.t: where K (source symbols per block) and interleave_depth are known from the catalog. Repair symbols have ESI >= K by convention: object_id 0 on the repair track corresponds to ESI K, object_id 1 to ESI K+1, and so on up to object_id P-1 (ESI K+P-1).
Publishers MUST use zero-based, monotonically-increasing group_id values on FEC-enabled tracks so that the SBN derivation is valid.

section.name: Repair Track Group Structure
section.t: Publishers MUST structure repair tracks so that group_id equals the Source Block Number (SBN). Object IDs within each repair group MUST be sequential starting from 0, where object_id R corresponds to repair symbol ESI K+R. This convention enables receivers to derive SBN and ESI from MoQ framing without per-object metadata, regardless of the source track's container format (LOC, CMAF, etc.).

This convention holds for all container modes. While CMAF source objects require explicit FEC Source Info extensions () because CMAF chunks do not map 1:1 to source symbols, repair objects ARE 1:1 with symbols (each object = one T-byte repair symbol), so the derivation is valid.
Publishers MUST publish exactly one repair symbol per MoQ object. The number of repair symbols per block (P) is known from the catalog.

section.name: Repair Symbol Generation
section.t: For RaptorQ, repair symbols are generated per Section 5.3. The Encoding Symbol ID (ESI) for repair symbols starts at K (the number of source symbols).
For Reed-Solomon, repair symbols are generated per using the Vandermonde matrix construction.
For XOR, a single repair symbol is generated as:
section.artwork: repair_symbol = source_symbol[0] XOR source_symbol[1] XOR ... XOR source_symbol[K-1]

section.name: LOC Repair Format
section.t: When the repair track's packaging is "loc", each MoQ object on the repair track is a standard LOC object containing one repair symbol as its payload. SBN and ESI are derived from MoQ transport framing identically to the condensed format ():
section.artwork: SBN = floor(group_id / interleave_depth) ESI = K + object_id
section.t: No FEC-specific extension headers are needed on repair objects.

section.name: MoQ Extension Header for FEC
section.t: This section defines a single MoQ transport extension header used by FEC-enabled streams. This extension uses the MoQ transport extension header mechanism defined in . Presence is determined by the catalog -- no per-object flags byte is needed.
This extension is NOT needed when each MoQ object contains exactly one source symbol (object payload <= T bytes). This applies to:

ul.li: MMTP source objects (MMTP-level fragmentation produces symbol-sized packets)
LOC audio source objects (audio frames are typically <= T)
All repair objects (each object = one T-byte symbol, SBN/ESI from framing per )

section.t: This extension IS REQUIRED when a single MoQ object spans multiple source symbols (object payload > T bytes). This applies to:

ul.li: LOC video source objects (video frames are typically >> T)
CMAF source objects (fMP4 chunks are typically >> T)

section.t: For CMAF packaging , the concatenated byte stream of chunk payloads (moof+mdat) within a source block is divided into T-byte source symbols. The FEC Source Info on each CMAF source object identifies the first ESI of that chunk and optionally carries the original chunk size for zero-pad stripping.

section.name: FEC Source Info Extension
section.artwork: FEC Source Info Extension { Extension Type (i) = 0xNN, Length (i), // 8 or 8 + varint_size Source Block Number (32), Encoding Symbol ID (32), [Original Object Length (i)], // Present when Length > 8 }
section.t: The FEC Source Info extension carries the Source Block Number (SBN, 32 bits) and Encoding Symbol ID (ESI, 32 bits) for a total of 8 bytes minimum.

When Length > 8, the remaining bytes contain the Original Object Length as a QUIC variable-length integer (1-8 bytes, per Section 16). The receiver uses this to strip zero-padding from the last FEC source symbol of the object.

Publishers SHOULD include the Original Object Length for clean transport-layer object boundary detection. Publishers MAY omit it (Length = 8) when the media format provides reliable in-band framing (e.g., ISOBMFF box headers for CMAF, NAL unit length prefixes for LOC/AVCC).

section.name: Interleaving
section.t: Interleaving spreads source symbols across time to protect against burst loss. With interleave depth D, symbols from D consecutive media units are grouped into one source block. The SBN is computed as floor(Group_ID / D). Repair objects for block N are published after the last source object of Group (N+1) * D - 1.

section.name: Priority and Congestion
section.t: Repair tracks SHOULD use lower priority than source tracks so that repair data is dropped first under congestion.
Recommended priority assignment:
Under congestion, this separation ensures source media is preserved while repair overhead is gracefully shed.

When using the Datagram forwarding preference per , QUIC datagrams lack stream-level priority. MoQ implementations SHOULD implement application-level scheduling that respects the priority model above when multiplexing source and repair datagrams.

section.name: Security Considerations
section.t: FEC does not provide confidentiality -- it operates on whatever data it receives (plaintext or ciphertext). Integrity protection of repair tracks is RECOMMENDED to prevent attackers from causing incorrect source reconstruction. The repair overhead (P/K) SHOULD be limited to reasonable values (e.g., <= 50%) to prevent bandwidth amplification.

End-to-end content authentication on MoQ unicast MAY be provided by Secure Objects which provides object-level encryption and authentication. For multicast delivery, authentication mechanisms are defined in Section 6.

section.name: FEC Algorithm Registry
section.t: This document requests creation of a "MoQ FEC Algorithms" registry with the following initial values:
New registrations require Specification Required policy.

section.name: MoQ Streaming Format Packaging
section.t: This document requests registration of a MoQ Streaming Format packaging value in the registry established by :

The "condensed" packaging is used for repair tracks paired with CMAF or LOC source tracks, where the source packaging does not have native FEC repair framing. Each MoQ object contains one raw repair symbol with SBN/ESI derived from MoQ transport framing ().
Note: The "mmtp" packaging value is registered by . The "loc" packaging value is registered by . Repair tracks using those packagings follow the respective format's native FEC framing.

section.name: MoQ Object Extension Header Types
section.t: This document requests registration of the following MoQ object extension header type in the "MoQ Extension Header Types" registry (note: these are object-level extensions per ):

Note: The value 0xNN is a placeholder. Actual value is to be assigned in coordination with the MOQ WG. Implementations SHOULD use a value in the experimental range (0x38-0x3F for 1-byte encoding) until IANA allocation is confirmed.

front.title: Key words for use in RFCs to Indicate Requirement Levels
RaptorQ Forward Error Correction Scheme for Object Delivery
Ambiguity of Uppercase vs Lowercase in RFC 2119 Key Words
QUIC: A UDP-Based Multiplexed and Secure Transport
CMSF: A CMAF Compliant Implementation of MOQT Streaming Format
MPEG Media Transport (MMT) Packaging for Media over QUIC
Multicast Delivery and Endpoint Discovery for Media over QUIC
Secure Objects for Media over QUIC
Signaling, Delivery, Synchronization, and Error Protection
MMT-Based Media Transport Scheme in Digital Broadcasting Systems
Information technology - High efficiency coding and media delivery in heterogeneous environments - Part 1: MPEG media transport (MMT)

section.name: Recovery Example
section.t: Subscriber receives source objects 0,1,3,4,5,6,7,8,9 (object 2 lost) and repair objects 0,1,2.
With K=10, P=3, subscriber has:
RaptorQ decoder can recover the missing source symbol (ESI 2).

section.name: Per-Object Overhead Comparison
section.t: The following table compares per-object FEC signaling overhead across container formats on MoQ unicast.

tr.td: Audio frames <= T; SBN/ESI derived from MoQ coordinates
Video frames >> T; FEC Source Info (SBN+ESI)
FEC Source Info with optional Object Length
FEC Source Info with optional Object Length
MMTP Header (12) + FEC Payload ID (9) + OTI (12)

section.t: LOC audio and condensed repair formats derive SBN/ESI from MoQ coordinates (zero FEC overhead). LOC video and CMAF source objects require explicit FEC Source Info extensions (8 bytes minimum) because media objects exceed the symbol size T. The optional Object Length field adds 1-4 bytes for transport-layer zero-pad stripping. MMTP carries full per-packet OTI (33 bytes) for self-describing operation and ATSC 3.0 compatibility.

section.name: ATSC 3.0 Compatibility
section.t: This specification is designed for interoperability with ATSC A/331 Application-Layer FEC.

tr.td: OTI derived from catalog fec fields
Derived from Interleave Depth x frame duration

section.t: Publishers ingesting ATSC 3.0 broadcasts SHOULD preserve the original FEC parameters and pass them through in the catalog fec fields.
For ATSC 3.0 MMT mode ingest, the repair track uses "mmtp" packaging -- repair packets are passed through as-is ().

section.name: MMTP Repair Packaging (Informational)
section.t: NOTE: This appendix is informational. It creates no normative dependency on . Implementations that do not interwork with MMTP sources need not implement this packaging type.

section.name: Motivation
section.t: MMTP repair packaging enables FEC interoperability between MoQ CDN delivery and MMTP-based broadcast systems (SSM/ASM multicast, ATSC 3.0 MMT mode). Repair payloads are unmodified MMTP packets per , so receivers apply identical recovery regardless of path.

section.name: Wire Format
section.t: When the repair track's packaging is "mmtp", each MoQ repair object payload contains one complete MMTP repair packet:

section.name: Receiver Processing
section.t: The receiver extracts FEC parameters by reading at fixed byte offsets:
section.artwork: offset 0: MMTP Header (12 bytes) -- skip offset 12: SS_Start (3 bytes, big-endian) -> Source Block Number offset 15: RSB_length (3 bytes) -- skip offset 18: RS_ID (3 bytes) -> Encoding Symbol ID derivation offset 21: OTI (12 bytes) -> RFC 6330 Common + Scheme-Specific OTI offset 21: Transfer Length F (5 bytes, 40-bit big-endian) offset 26: Reserved (1 byte) offset 27: Symbol Size T (2 bytes, big-endian) offset 29: Num Source Blocks Z (1 byte) offset 30: Num Sub-Blocks N (2 bytes) offset 32: Alignment Al (1 byte) offset 33: Repair Symbol Data (T bytes) -> memcpy to decoder
section.t: K (source symbols per block) is derived: K = ceil(F / T).

section.name: Per-Packet OTI
section.t: OTI is carried in every MMTP repair packet at offset 21 (12 bytes), enabling mid-stream join without catalog and supporting mixed-K streams on a shared repair track.

section.name: Relay Behavior
section.t: Relays SHOULD pass MMTP repair packets through unmodified -- no header stripping, re-framing, or re-encoding.

section.name: Relationship to Catalog
section.t: Catalog fec fields are OPTIONAL when packaging is "mmtp" (OTI is per-packet). If present, they SHOULD match per-packet OTI values and are useful for repair track discovery before the first packet.
